title: Suddenly Popular
alt: Are Your Teens Practicing Amplexus? Learn These Six Telltale Signs!
transcript:
Obscure words and phrases everyone suddenly becomes very familiar with.
[A time line to the left is marked of by several phrases to the right around the time they became familiar to the public:]
← World Wide Web
← DNA Evidence
1995
← Militia Movement
← Supermax
2000
← Butterfly Ballot
← Al-Qaeda
← Wi-Fi
2005
← Tsunami
← Viral
2010
← Radicalize
← Metadata
2015
← Lahar
← Insect-Borne
2020
← Earth-Crossing
← Thermohaline
← Snow-Blindness
2025
← Amplexus
← Controlled Hydroplaning
2030
← Paradoxical Reaction
← Drone Desertion
← Rapid Hair Growth
2035
← I Swear Allegiance To The God-Empress In Life And In Death
2040
explanation:
Many phrases that used to be of mainly academic interest become popular when an important event or global trend is described with such phrases in the media. Randall presents a timeline of past examples, and predicts phrases that may be popularised in the near future. The past events are a mix of buzz words and words that became popularized as a result of technology trends, natural disasters, or terrorism. The future events seem to be all related to natural disasters or other kinds of serious issues, except  — which is the joke of the title text — showing that no matter how many disasters there are, people are generally more concerned about their teenagers' sex lives.

The title text is also an example of a  headline. Many organizations will post a link on social media to their content with a sensationalized headline in order to draw readers in. In this case, the headline is geared towards parents who are worried about their children being sexually active in this new Amplexus way. Such headlines are the internet's analog to television news'  ("A new trend among teens is sweeping the nation, but is it dangerous? Details at 11:00.").

 is a theme throughout this comic. Randall predicts a large asteroid impact/near miss and a volcanic eruption, followed by an  or . An insect borne, global pandemic without a cure also strikes, and then the  occurs.

This comic has similar features to 887: Future Timeline.

===List of the phrases===
Below the phrases are listed with the closest year from the time-line noted behind the phrase. Note that this year does not necessarily match with the in-real-life relevant year. This may be found in the explanation of the phrase below. Google Books Ngram Viewer can show the relative frequency of those words in function of the year.

; – 1994
:Though first proposed in 1989, and the first test being completed in 1990, it took until around 1994 for the  to start becoming well known.

; – 1995
:Prominent coverage of the  in 1994 brought widespread discussion of , making it famous and showing its limitations.

; – 1996
:After the standoffs at , Idaho in 1992 and the  compound in Waco, Texas between U.S. Government Agencies and militias in 1993, people started becoming more aware of their presence, culminating with the 1995  on the second anniversary of the fire at the Branch Davidians compound.

; – 1997
:Super-Maximum security prisons. Possibly referring to the 1997 film , starring Nicolas Cage, John Cusack, and John Malkovich, in which prisoners being transferred to a new Supermax prison seize control of their transport plane. Also possibly referring to  and , perpetrators of the aforementioned Oklahoma City bombing, who were incarcerated at , the former from the time of his arrest to 1999, and the latter from the time of his conviction to the present day.


; – 2000
:In the  had a major recount dispute that took center stage in the election. Thus, the outcome of the 2000 United States presidential election was not known for more than a month after balloting, because of the extended process of counting and then recounting of Florida presidential ballots. The  was the type of ballot design , and was a central issue in the election controversy. Evidence suggests that many voters who intended to vote for Gore or Bush actually marked their ballots for Pat Buchanan or spoiled their ballots, because of a confusing layout of the ballot.

; – 2002
:The  brought the al-Qaeda terrorist organisation into the spotlight almost overnight.

; – 2003
:Wi-Fi, though developed in the 1990s, first became popular in the early 2000s.

; – 2006
:There were a number of tsunamis around this time period, in particular the  which caused 230,000 deaths, and the . These were some of the first tsunamis to be widely captured on camera, bringing these previously obscure seismic events into the public eye. 

; – 2009
:In this context, the word viral is used to describe anything which spreads rapidly and widely on the internet. In particular an online video clip is said to have 'Gone Viral' or become a  if it racks up a high number of views over a short time. This phenomenon has become especially prevalent due to users sharing content on .

; – 2011
:Due to the ongoing , and the relative ease with which one can travel from Europe to Syria by way of Turkey, there is growing concern about the risk of young Muslims in Europe (and, to a lesser extent, the United States) becoming  by indoctrination from fundamentalists either in their communities or on the Internet.

; – 2013
:Following the highly publicised 2013 leaks by  of information regarding the 's indiscriminate surveillance of global communication metadata, awareness of the privacy value of such data became widespread, where once it was mostly familiar to IT professionals.


From this point on, phrases were in the future at the time of publication.


; – 2016
:A lahar is a mudslide caused by the eruption of a volcano that was covered with snow or ice. Randall is speculating on a future natural disaster being caused by such an incident.  volcano covered with the  glacier on Iceland increased activity just a few days before publishing of this comic, but its eventual  caused little harm.

; – 2019
:Some , meaning specific species of insects are the main vector in spreading to humans.  is an example of an . Randall predicts some severe (possibly deadly) insect-borne disease will emerge around this time. As of 2016, the Zika virus, which is transmitted by mosquitoes, has reached epidemic status in South America, and has spread to southern North America, Africa and Australia. 2019 was the year of the first COVID-19 outbreak, leading to a pandemic in 2020, so Randall's prediction of a major disease could be accurate; however, COVID-19 is not an insect-borne disease. "Coronavirus" would be the formerly obscure term instead.

; – 2021
:Earth-crossers are asteroids that cross the orbit of Earth. Most of them remain harmless because their orbit doesn't actually intersect the earth's orbit in 3 dimensions, or for the foreseeable future, they will cross when Earth is not there. For this phrase to become popular, an Earth-crosser might have to reach the heretofore-unreached "threatening" level, rating a 5 or more on the , due to a significant chance of a large impact. In 2014, there were no threats of that level known in the early 2020s. If the next two phrases are connected to this one, Randall predicted a significant asteroid impact.

; – 2022
:Thermohaline circulation is the largest group of interconnected ocean currents, which stabilize global climate by equalizing the temperature and salinity of oceans around the world. If this phrase becomes popular, it implies the thermohaline circulation would have slowed or changed significantly. This might be caused by asteroid impact or by polar ice melting. The latter scenario was apocalyptically dramatized in the movie .

; – 2024
:Snow blindness is an eye condition caused by excessive UV light reflected from snow and ice. This can lead to corneal damage and blindness (temporary, if treated properly). This phrase becoming popular might suggest a long  (from the asteroid) or severe  in cold regions of the world.

; – 2025
:A form of non-penetrative reproduction carried out by some animals, for example frogs, involving grasping the partner with front legs. This may be connected to the other posts (some change in human society) or it may simply be a joke at how new sexual language/fads appear and hit mainstream media from time to time (for example a number of acts gained fame from Sex and the City). This also ties in with the title text, which imagines a sensationalist headline suggesting teenagers may be doing this.

; – 2028
:Hydroplaning occurs when a vehicle tire comes in contact with a puddle in such a way that the water builds up between the tire and the road surface. The film of water, having a much lower coefficient of friction than the road surface, causes the tire to lose traction. Typically, in this scenario, the driver isn't planning to hydroplane and loses control of the vehicle. In theory, controlled hydroplaning would be achieved when the driver plans for it ahead of time. This could be necessary if, in this hypothetical future, most of the roads are flooded since the impact winter (after only four years) ends and thus a great thaw causes all roads to become wet all the time.

; – 2031
:"Paradoxical reaction" is a medical term for when the outcome of a medical treatment, typically the taking of a drug, is the opposite of that expected. For example if taking a pain relieving medication made the pain worse. For this term to suddenly become well known, a large scale or particularly notable case must have taken place (such as the insect-borne disease of 2019). 
:Alternatively, this and the following (and maybe the last as well) phrases may refer to the events from  by Isaac Asimov (and its very loose but much more popular film adaptation ) where robots, instructed with the , take over the world to prevent humans hurting each other. The paradoxical reaction is that these laws were specifically designed to, among other things, prevent robots from taking over the world in the first place. See also: 1613: The Three Laws of Robotics.

; – 2033
:Desertion is the abandonment of a post or duty, usually military in nature. With the increasing use of autonomous drones by the military this hints at an event where drones 'decide' to desert, possibly due to unspecified advances in  and . Or maybe they just start following the . See also: 2499: Abandonment Function, and 1626: Judgment Day.

; – 2034
:Maybe some humans have developed a very rapid hair growth (presumably on the entire body) through evolution and natural selection caused by the cold years of the impact winter.

; – 2038
:This is a . A possible explanation is that after the impact and the desertion of the drones predicted for 2033, a strong fraction has made their leader divine, and everyone now has to swear allegiance to this new God-Empress using this phrase - which would certainly make it a very "popular" phrase. The phrase God-Emperor was popularized in the science-fiction work Dune in 1965 and has been repeatedly referenced since, notably in the tabletop game Warhammer 40,000 (and related media). The God-Empress is later referenced in 2661: Age Milestone Privileges.


As an additional note for further consideration, almost all the ‘future’ events appear to be references to the webcomic Homestuck. The webcomic features a version of Earth which would undergo all the cataclysmic events described above; the insect-borne disease could be a reference to the fact that the comic’s version of Earth would be invaded by an insect-based race of aliens called “Trolls,” who would ostensibly carry their own set of diseases (although that’s not a plot point in the comic, one would assume the aliens carried their own set of pathogens); the Earth-crossing asteroid bit references the fact that the main characters play a game that causes asteroids to bombard the earth (the thermohaline and snow-blindness parts are simply the logical scientific extensions of the phenomenon); amplexus could refer to the fact that the trolls reproduce externally (though in more of a queen-bee/drones kind of way than frogs (but the idea of frogs as vessels of reproduction is also broached in the comic, so double points I guess); controlled hydroplaning references the fact that the invading troll empress would go on to terraform the earth for herself by entirely flooding it; the paradoxical reaction references the game-world the characters get transferred to, called Paradox Space; the drone desertion could reference the troll species’ drones, robots that aid in troll reproduction and otherwise serve their empress; and finally, the allegiance-swearing to the “god empress” refers to the troll empress, “Her Imperious Condescension,” who would go on to take over the earth in-comic. Furthermore, this comic was #1413, and the number 413 holds great symbolic importance in Homestuck. And as a matter of fact, Munroe himself lists Homestuck as a comic he likes, which really supports this interpretation.
